chapters.title: Transition is a make-or-break point for new leaders
chapters.text:
During your transition and beyond, make it a priority to secure early wins. Doing this will give you credibility and momentum; people will know that you're indeed putting in the work, and there are lots of good things to show for it.

To kick things off effectively, you should try to start simple.

However, you must be very careful not to focus on irrelevant projects. Many leaders often fall victim to what Michael Watkins calls the “low-hanging fruit trap” by expending their energy on securing early wins that have very little to do with their ultimate goal at the organization.

When you were appointed leader, there were some milestones you, your boss, and company stakeholders agreed that you would reach during your tenure. For instance, you might have been charged with increasing the organization's annual revenue, improving the quality of products, or getting certain projects done within specified periods.

So the wins you achieve during your transition should be meaningful to contribute to your long-term goals and simple enough to help you build momentum in the short term. Pinpoint the most promising opportunities and put all your efforts into translating them into wins, one at a time.

Early wins build your credibility and create momentum. ~ Michael Watkins

Additionally, modifying any challenging behavioral patterns among your team members can assist in reaching your primary objective within the set time. First, identify the behaviors; these might include a lack of focus, innovation, discipline, sense of urgency, and teamwork. Once you've discovered any pattern within the group that can hold you back, you can then create a vision for how you would like the people to conduct themselves by the end of your tenure. Finally, work out a plan on how your actions to pursue early wins will facilitate the process.

chapters.title: Each role on the corporate career ladder is unique
chapters.text:
One of the biggest mistakes a new leader can make is to assume that what made them successful in the past will continue to do so. To position yourself for success, mentally disengage from your previous position and ready yourself to assume control of your new role.

You might think that the reason you got this promotion was due to your outstanding performance in your previous role. Perhaps, you are coming in afresh from another organization where you were the star employee. Whatever the circumstances that led to your appointment to a leadership position, you must endeavor not to approach your new role with the same tactics that have brought you success in the past.

As a new leader, it's crucial to concentrate on the fundamental guidelines for preparing for your new position.

The first thing you want to do to prepare for the role is to establish a clear breaking point. In most industries, a move from one position to another always happens very quickly. People rarely get a clean transition. You might find yourself thrust into a new position with little to no time to prepare for the task ahead. So it is important to take some time to take a mental break away from your old job. Find time on a weekend, sit somewhere quiet, and imagine yourself letting go of your previous position and embracing the new one with joy and zeal.

Next, assess your strengths and weaknesses. One way to figure your vulnerabilities is to think about the kind of problems you enjoy solving the most. Some people like solving technical problems, such as issues that have to do with technologies, markets, and strategies. Some like solving cultural problems that involve norms and values, while others enjoy solving problems that involve power and politics in the organization.

Whatever kinds of issues you enjoy handling, you've probably perfected your skill in solving such problems. Unfortunately, focusing solely on one skill set makes you weak when you find yourself in a position that requires ambidexterity. To make up for your vulnerabilities, discipline yourself and find the time to improve your abilities in critical activities that you do not enjoy. Also, look for people in your company who are good in those areas and learn from them.

chapters.title: Match your strategies with your company
chapters.text:
As a newly appointed leader, you are going to find yourself in one of five kinds of situations:

• Initial Launch
• Turnaround
• Rapid Radid growth
• Realignment
• Maintaining achievement

You will need to tailor your strategies depending on where your organization is on this STARS model.

To be successful, you need to mobilize the energy of many others in your organization. ~ Michael D. Watkins

In all of the five situations, the principal goal is to turn the business into a successful and thriving one. However, each scenario comes with different challenges. In a start-up, you will be challenged to assemble the capabilities to launch a new product or project. In turnarounds, your main aim is to get a washed-up unit or team back on track.

In an accelerated growth situation, the company will be experiencing a rapid expansion, so you will be charged with putting in the systems and processes necessary to accommodate the growth. For realignment, your job is to re-energize a once-successful business or project that is now in a precarious situation. And in sustenance, you're taking over a company that is already performing well, so your challenge is to maintain a high standard while working on ways to take the organization to the next level.

So you must understand the kind of situation you are inheriting during your transition.

To take an organization forward, you have to know its origin, where it currently is, and where it's going.

You cannot figure out how to improve a company if you don't know where it fits in the STARS matrix. Various scenarios demand appropriate alterations in how you plan and execute your transition. By having a clear understanding of the situation at hand, you will be able to create the right action plan to help you get the result you need.

chapters.title: Secure early, meaningful wins
chapters.text:
During your transition and beyond, make it a priority to secure early wins. Doing this will give you credibility and momentum; people will know that you're indeed putting in the work, and there are lots of good things to show for it.

To kick things off effectively, you should try to start simple.

However, you must be very careful not to focus on irrelevant projects. Many leaders often fall victim to what Michael Watkins calls the “low-hanging fruit trap” by expending their energy on securing early wins that have very little to do with their ultimate goal at the organization.

When you were appointed leader, there were some milestones you, your boss, and company stakeholders agreed that you would reach during your tenure. For instance, you might have been charged with increasing the organization's annual revenue, improving the quality of products, or getting certain projects done within specified periods.

So the wins you achieve during your transition should be meaningful to contribute to your long-term goals and simple enough to help you build momentum in the short term. Pinpoint the most promising opportunities and put all your efforts into translating them into wins, one at a time.

Early wins build your credibility and create momentum. ~ Michael Watkins

Additionally, modifying any challenging behavioral patterns among your team members can assist in reaching your primary objective within the set time. First, identify the behaviors; these might include a lack of focus, innovation, discipline, sense of urgency, and teamwork. Once you've discovered any pattern within the group that can hold you back, you can then create a vision for how you would like the people to conduct themselves by the end of your tenure. Finally, work out a plan on how your actions to pursue early wins will facilitate the process.

chapters.title: Build a team that can work together
chapters.text:
As a leader, everyone on your team is vital to your success. You cannot actualize your ultimate vision for the organization by yourself; you need to surround yourself with the right people.

If you work with the right team during your transition and beyond, you will achieve your goals much more easily.

Depending on where your company is on the STARS matrix, you will either be building a team from scratch or inheriting one. Whatever the situation is, it is important that you assess every member of your team before taking them on board based on the following six criteria:

• Competence. Do they possess the necessary skills and knowledge?
• Relationships. Do they have good relationships with other members?
• Trust. Can you rely on this person to consistently fulfill their promises and commitments?
• Judgment. Do they exercise good judgment?
• Energy. Do they bring the right energy to the team?
• Focus. How focused is this member of the team?

Divide 100 points between the criteria according to how important each of them is to you. For instance, you may assign 10 points to trust and 20 points to competence. Set a minimum score each of the people you are evaluating must get before bringing them aboard. If you've inherited a team and any of its members fail to meet your threshold requirements, start working on replacing them as soon as possible.

After you've evaluated each person individually, take time to study the team as a whole. During your early meetings, pay attention to the group dynamic. How do the members interact with one another? Can you sense any conflict or coalition? Knowing how well the team functions will better enable you to lead it effectively.

Once you have the right people in place, bring them up to speed about your vision for the organization and let each person know how they fit into your plan and what role they have to play.

chapters.title: Surround yourself with influential and supportive people
chapters.text:
If you want to win big as a manager, you must create alliances during the first few months of your tenure. You can not achieve your dream for the organization by solely depending on authority.

Even if your position comes with considerable authority, there will still be some people over whom you have no sufficient influence, and there will come a time when you need their support. So a reasonable part of your first 90 days should be dedicated to identifying key personnel and swing voters who are likely to support your initiatives. This is essential for realizing your early-win objectives.

To realize your managerial goals, you have to cultivate relationships with those who aren’t directly under your control.

To get started, take time to figure out why you need other people’s support. Identify the alliances necessary for securing your initial successes. Determine which of these triumphs require the support of individuals outside your direct influence. With a clear goal in mind, you can then identify the key individuals whose backing is essential to achieve your objectives.

So how do you create your network of influence?

According to Michael Watkins, one of the best strategies you can use is to ask your boss to introduce you to key stakeholders. Get a list of the most influential people in the organization and set up a meeting with each of them.

Also, pay attention during general meetings to see whose opinions almost always hold on important issues. Whose advice and insight do people tend to seek? Who do people direct their attention to when crucial issues are raised? As you learn more, you will start to understand the power dynamics of the organization.

Knowing how the main shot callers coalesce to pursue certain goals will help you figure out their agendas and structure your plans in line with them. This can give you tremendous power and support within the organization. Just be careful not to get sucked into unnecessary office politics and lose sight of your ultimate agenda.

Did you know? There will be people who will form a “blocking alliance” against you. They will band together to thwart your agenda at any opportunity they get. Knowing who these people are and how they might boycott you can help you work on neutralizing their opposition.

chapters.title: Manage yourself well during transition and beyond
chapters.text:
During your transition, you will face a lot of personal and professional tumult. You are newly appointed, you've not had the opportunity to build a support network, and there is already a great expectation for you to get things going and start effecting change as soon as possible. Things might even be tougher if you've had to move your whole family to a different city for the job.

In the face of everything, however, it is important that you keep your balance. You must work hard to preserve your ability to make good judgments.

Here are some common mistakes that throw most leaders off the track at the onset of their tenure:

• Staying in isolation
• Not focusing
• Avoiding important tasks
• Failing to establish solid boundaries
• Shying away from making tough decisions
• Being rigid and getting defensive after making wrong judgment calls

If you find yourself exhibiting any of the dysfunctional behaviors listed above, work on eliminating them with immediate effect. Develop personal discipline by creating regular routines in line with your goals and enforcing them on yourself ruthlessly.

Also, devote time to planning your weeks beforehand, and take at least 10 minutes at the end of every day to evaluate how well you met your goals for the day and then plan for the next day. Lastly, build and solidify your support network by asserting control in your local environment.

It is important that you establish balance in your life.

In essence, managing yourself is vital during your transition. The enthusiasm you felt when you first got the job will wane at some point, and the reality of the challenge you took on will set in. No matter how overwhelming things get, however, you must maintain your energy and perspective and focus on the right things the right way.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Many leaders inadvertently put their careers and the goals they have for their organization in jeopardy because of a series of poor decisions they made during their first few days on the job. Transition is a crucial point in any leader’s career. Everything you do during your first 90 days in office will determine whether or not you will have a successful tenure. A failed transition can throw a newly appointed manager into a vicious cycle and ultimately mark the end of their career.

Therefore, your objective during the transition is to swiftly reach the break-even point. This is the point where you start making decisions that add value to your organization. You can only start contributing to the company when you begin to take action to effect change.

As much as you might like to reach the break-even point on time, however, you shouldn’t rush to make changes when you get started. Yes, you might have been great in your previous role, but that doesn’t necessarily mean that the tactics that worked for you then will work now.

You must tailor your strategy to the organization’s level of growth. It is also imperative that you take time to disconnect mentally from the position you just left and embrace the current one. Study and analyze your new working environment and co-workers. Get a feel for what works at the company and figure out which small wins you can quickly secure to build up momentum that will propel you throughout your tenure.

Finally, the people you choose to work with also have a tremendous effect on your success. Before getting started, build a team of people that are highly competent and well-aligned with your ultimate vision for the organization. If you’ve inherited a team, make sure you restructure it to suit your tactics.

Try this

To make a successful transition and build credibility for a successful tenure, make sure you:

• Mentally prepare to take charge in your new role.
• Use the STARS model to match your strategy to the company's situation.
• Identify at least three key areas where you can achieve early improvements that align with your ultimate goal for the organization.
• Recognize individuals whose backing is crucial for your success and establish partnerships with them.
• Achieve balance in your life by creating and enforcing personal disciplines.

Read it till the end?
